title: Filling a crack in a concrete floor
subtitle: Repair hairline to medium-width cracks in a concrete slab floor
excerpt: How to repair hairline, shrinkage, and medium-width cracks in a domestic concrete floor using grout or a flexible floor-repair compound, including cleaning the crack, undercutting for key, priming, and feathering the repair flush with the surrounding slab.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Larsen Building Products technical guide. British Standard BS 8204 screeded and concrete floors.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fine crack that develops in a concrete slab as the concrete dries and cures. Shrinkage cracks are typically hairline width (less than 0.3 mm), random in direction, and confined to the surface. They do not indicate structural movement.

A crack in a floor slab that indicates movement in the structure below — subsidence, heave, or overloading. Structural cracks typically run in a consistent direction, are wider than 1 mm, are accompanied by differential settlement (one side of the crack higher than the other), and grow over time. Structural cracks are beyond the scope of a DIY surface repair.

Widening a crack or hole at its base so that the repair material can key mechanically into the substrate. A crack that is wider at the base than the surface holds the repair compound in place better than a straight-sided crack.

content.text: Before starting any repair, classify the crack. A
is a cosmetic problem: a DIY surface repair is the appropriate response. A

means the building is moving: filling the surface will not solve anything, and a structural engineer's advice is needed before any repair is attempted. If the crack is wider at one end than the other, if the floor is stepped at the crack (one side higher), or if the crack has appeared suddenly or grown since you last looked, have it assessed before touching it.
Step 1, clean and widen the crack

Use the tip of the utility knife or a cold chisel to clean loose material out of the crack. For hairline cracks, open the crack to at least 5 mm width with a cold chisel and club hammer: a crack narrower than 5 mm is too tight for filler to pack into properly.

the crack by angling the chisel so that the base of the crack is wider than the surface opening. Brush out all dust and debris. Vacuum or blow out any remaining material with a compressed-air duster. The repair will fail if there is dust between the filler and the concrete.

For a concrete repair compound or mortar, dampen the crack with water and let it reach a damp but not wet state. This stops dry concrete drawing moisture out of the repair compound too fast. For a polyurethane or epoxy filler, follow the manufacturer's priming instructions. Most require a brush coat of the liquid component into the crack before packing in the filler.

For hairline cracks that will be tiled over: a cement-based floor-levelling compound or patching mortar is sufficient. For cracks that will be left exposed (polished concrete or painted floor): use a polyurethane or epoxy crack filler, which sets harder, sands cleaner, and does not dust. For active cracks (those that may continue to move slightly with seasonal temperature change): use a flexible polyurethane sealant rather than a rigid filler. A rigid fill in an active crack will pop out within a year.
Step 4, pack and overfill the crack

Press the repair compound into the crack using the 50 mm filling knife, working along the crack length and ensuring the material is packed into the undercut base. Leave the repair slightly proud of the surrounding floor surface to allow for any minor settlement. For a deep crack, build up in two or more layers with drying time between them: a deep single fill is prone to shrinkage cracking at the centre.

Once the repair is cured (follow the material's data sheet: typically 4 to 24 hours depending on product and temperature), use the 100 mm filling knife to scrape any proud areas flush. For a polished or painted finish, sand the repaired area with 60-grit and progress to 120-grit on the sanding block, feathering the edge into the surrounding floor. Apply the floor paint or concrete sealer to the whole floor to blend the repair colour.

For a crack directly under a floating floor (laminate or engineered wood), fill with a thin flexible levelling compound and relay the floor: provided the crack has stopped moving. For a garage floor crack that takes vehicle loads, use an epoxy crack filler rated for traffic. Standard mortar will not handle the repeated load and will fracture again within a season.

attrs.symptom: Repair compound cracks again or pops out shortly after installation
attrs.cause: Crack is active and still moving; or a rigid fill was used in a dynamic crack.
attrs.fix: Remove the filler and reassess the crack. If the crack is still growing, a structural engineer should be consulted. If it is an active but non-structural crack, use a flexible polyurethane sealant.

attrs.symptom: Repair is crumbly and soft after curing
attrs.cause: Too much water added to a cement-based mix; repair applied to a very wet surface.
attrs.fix: Rake out the weak fill, allow the crack to dry thoroughly, and reapply with a correctly mixed compound.

attrs.symptom: Visible colour difference between repair and surrounding floor
attrs.cause: Different material, different aggregate, or different cure time affecting colour.
attrs.fix: Apply a concrete floor paint or sealer to the whole floor to unify the colour. Spot-colouring only the repair area will read as a patch.
